did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model called FourierCNN, which integrates Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), to classify benign and malignant skin cancer images from the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning model (FourierCNN) for skin cancer classification. It details a specific methodology (integrating FFT with CNN), describes the dataset used (ISIC 2017), reports experimental results (83.79% accuracy, 0.93 AUC), and compares these new results against prior works. Although published in a conference proceedings (IATMSI), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing existing literature.

title: FourierCNN: Skin cancer Classification using Convolution Neural Network Fortified with Fast Fourier Transform
journal: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
brief_summary: This study proposes FourierCNN, a deep learning model that integrates Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify benign and malignant skin cancer images. The model was trained and evaluated using the ISIC 2017 dataset, which consisted of 3,297 images divided into training and test sets. The primary evaluation approach involved assessing performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Area Under the Curve (AUC). The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 83.79% and an AUC of 0.93, demonstrating its effectiveness in leveraging both spatial and frequency domain features for skin lesion categorization.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used for training and testing. The dataset contained 360 benign samples (Table 2). The model achieved a precision of 0.89 and recall of 0.84 for this class.

One of the two binary classification classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used for training and testing. The dataset contained 300 malignant samples (Table 2). The model achieved a precision of 0.78 and recall of 0.89 for this class. Specifically, the abstract mentions distinguishing 'melanoma' images, implying melanoma is a primary malignant type within this category.

Mentioned in the abstract as a specific type of malignant skin cancer that the model distinguishes from benign lesions ('distinguished the melanoma and benign skin lesion images'). However, the experimental setup uses the binary 'malignant' label from the ISIC 2017 dataset rather than multi-class melanoma vs. other cancers.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer images into benign and malignant classes
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of benign and malignant skin cancer images' using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) with reported metrics including accuracy (83.79%), precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC (0.93). The confusion matrix and class-specific metrics confirm binary classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A collection of 3,297 skin lesion images provided by the official ISIC website, categorized into benign and malignant classes.

use.training: Used to train the FourierCNN model with data augmentation (random rotation of 10 degrees) and normalization.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; validation accuracy of 0.83 was noted during evaluation, but no specific validation set count is provided.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance, with 660 samples forming the test set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance by counting true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for benign and malignant classes.
To assess the model's ability to distinguish between benign and malignant classes across different threshold settings.
To quantify classification accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for both benign and malignant classes.
To compare the proposed model's accuracy and AUC against other existing methods (Jayapriya and Jacob, Yilmaz et al., Mahbod et al.) using the same dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC, and confusion matrix analysis, with comparative assessments against other works based on these metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.notes: The proposed FourierCNN model outperformed the comparative baselines (Jayapriya and Jacob, Yilmaz et al., Mahbod et al.) in terms of AUC. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 83.79% and an AUC of 0.93 on the ISIC 2017 dataset.

architecture_details.backbone: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)
architecture_details.fft_integration: Input images (R, G, B components) are transformed into frequency domain using FFT, splitting into real and imaginary parts

layers.description: Output from last subsampling layer is flattened

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via random rotation of 10 degrees
Image normalization to scale pixel values between 0 and 1
Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) applied to R, G, and B components to transform spatial information into frequency domain (splitting into real and imaginary parts)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: A random rotation of 10 degrees was applied to the input training dataset to provide variability.
Pixel range was normalized to 0 to 1 to ensure better convergence and generalisability.

conclusions: The proposed FourierCNN model, which integrates Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) with a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), effectively classifies benign and malignant skin cancer images by leveraging both spatial and frequency domain features.
The model achieved an accuracy of 83.79% and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.93 on the ISIC 2017 dataset, demonstrating robust performance.
The proposed method outperformed comparative baseline studies (Jayapriya and Jacob, Yilmaz et al., Mahbod et al.) in terms of AUC, indicating superior discriminative capability.

The integration of FFT allows the model to reveal distinguishing features in the frequency domain that may not be immediately apparent in the spatial domain, enhancing the identification of critical features for skin lesion categorization.

The study suggests that automated computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) systems using deep learning can help overcome the challenges of misdiagnosis by human experts and reduce the strain on healthcare resources by improving the accuracy and durability of skin cancer diagnoses.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC 2017 dataset, which contains a small total of 3,297 images, limiting the generalizability to multi-class melanoma subtypes or other datasets.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance with 360 benign samples versus 300 malignant samples in the test set, which may bias precision and recall metrics.

The model architecture is extremely shallow (only two convolutional layers with 32 and 128 filters) and uses a very low input resolution of 32x32 pixels, which may fail to capture fine-grained dermatological features compared to modern deep learning standards.
Critical training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, learning rate, number of epochs, and batch size are not reported, preventing reproducibility of the experimental results.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the ISIC 2017 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of robustness across different clinical populations.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the proposed FourierCNN and the comparative baseline models.
The comparative assessment relies on AUC values from prior works without ensuring identical data splits or preprocessing pipelines, making direct performance comparisons potentially invalid.
The paper contains a typographical error in Section I, referring to 'pneumonia classification' instead of skin cancer, indicating a lack of careful proofreading.
The reported accuracy of 83.79% is modest for this domain, and the model's performance is not significantly superior to existing baselines like Mahbod et al. (AUC 0.907) or Yilmaz et al. (AUC 0.905).
